coredata.dc:title: High-Energy-Density Li-O2 Battery at Cell Scale with Folded Cell Structure
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Heung Chan
Park, Jung Ock
Kim, Mokwon
Kwon, Hyuk Jae
Kim, Joon-Hee
Choi, Kyoung Hwan
Kim, Kihong
Im, Dongmin
coredata.dc:description: As potential successors to intercalation-based battery systems, Li-air batteries have attracted enormous research attention because of their higher theoretical specific energy compared with conventional batteries, in terms of unit of energy per active material mass. However, this unit complicates the estimation of realistically attainable cell-scale energy densities because all other indispensable cell components are excluded. Here, we report Li-O2 batteries with an ultra-thin, customized gas-diffusion layer, highly conductive, stable polymeric ionic-liquid separator, and folded cell structure that reduce the volume and mass of multiple cell components. A complete cell-scale specific energy and energy density of 1,214 Wh kgcell −1 and 896 Wh Lcell −1, respectively, were experimentally achieved. Cell-scale electrochemical phenomena were simulated using a multiphysics model that included the oxygen flow caused by the cathode reaction, dynamic porosity changes, and resistance increase during Li2O2 formation. Both the cell geometry and cathode composition dramatically affected the cell's capacity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-O2 battery
Li-air battery
cell-scale energy density
simulation
modeling
polymeric ionic liquid
separator
folded cell structure

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell Components, Structure, and Modeling Domains
Carbon Nanoparticle Composite Cathode: GDL Thickness and Cell Depth Effects
Maximum Cell-Scale Capacity Prediction for CNP Cathode
CNT Composite Cathode: Effect of Cathode Composition

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This article is made available under the Elsevier license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure and Images of a Single-Fold Cell
(A) Layered anode, separator, and cathode sheets are folded in half with a shared GDL in the middle. T, L, and D denote the height, length, and depth of the single-fold cell.
(B) Simulation domains with governing physical principles.
(C and D) Images of the folded cell (C) before and (D) after being folded.
Discharge Profiles of Single-Fold Cells with Varying Depths

(A and B) Discharge profiles of single-fold cells with L = 5 cm, D = 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 cm, and H = 340 μm obtained experimentally (A) and by simulation (B) are plotted. The discharge current is 0.24 mA cm−2. The GDL thickness is 180 μm with a CNP/ionic-liquid composite as the cathode and a polypropylene film infused with polymeric ionic-liquid electrolyte as separator. Li metal is used as the anode.

(C) Cell depths plotted versus the discharge capacities at 2.3 V. The experimental and simulated values are plotted. The linear regression line corresponds to the carbon-specific capacity of 3,200 mAh gcarbon −1.
Discharge Profiles of Single-Fold Cells with Varying Depths and a Custom-Modified GDL

(A and B) Discharge profiles of single-fold cells with L = 5 cm, D = 0.5, 1, 2, 3, and 8 cm, and H = 210 μm obtained experimentally (A) and by simulation (B) are plotted. The discharge current is 0.24 mA cm−2. A custom-modified GDL (50 μm thick) was used with all the same components as in Figure 2.

(C) Cell depths plotted versus discharge capacities at 2.3 V. The experimental and simulated values are plotted. The linear regression line corresponds to a carbon-specific capacity of 3,200 mAh gcarbon −1.
Simulation Results for Folded Cells at Various Depths of Discharge

(A) Oxygen mass fractions (
ω
O 2
) in GDLs and concentrations (
C
O 2
) in cathodes of cells with depths of 2 cm (left) and 8 cm (right), corresponding to their downward depth of discharge (DOD). Half of the GDL thickness is 25 μm and the cathode thickness is 30 μm.

(B) Magnitude of the current density (the arrow indicates the directions of the electrode current density at 100% DOD). The electrolyte current is always directed upward (not shown). The thicknesses of half of the GDL, the cathode, separator, and anode are 25, 30, 20, and 30 μm, respectively.
(C and D) Porosity and Li2O2 thickness (C) and surface conductivity (D) at the cathode in 2-cm (left) and 8-cm (right) cells, respectively.
Energy Densities and Specific Energies with Varying Cell Depths and Four GDL Thicknesses

(A and B) Plots of cell depth versus the (A) energy density and (B) specific energy obtained experimentally or through simulations for cells with varying GDL thicknesses. The solid points represent the simulated energy densities of cells with GDL thicknesses of 20, 50, 100, and 180 μm, whereas open squares and circles are experimentally obtained energy densities with GDL thicknesses of 50 and 180 μm, respectively. Gray solid and dotted lines are calculated theoretical limits by cathode pore volume and by surface passivation (9 nm Li2O2), respectively, when GDL thickness is 50 μm. The cell length is 5 cm. The discharge current is 0.24 mA cm−2.
Experimental and Simulated Discharge Profiles for Single-Fold Cells with Carbon Nanotube-Based Composite Cathodes

(A and B) Discharge profiles of single-fold cells with L = 3 cm, D = 1 cm, and 1.5, 2.0, and 3.0 mg cm−2 cathode loadings (H = 180, 190, and 210 μm) obtained from (A) experiments and (B) simulations. GDL, 50 μm thick; cathode, CNTs/ionic-liquid composite; separator, polypropylene film saturated with polymeric ionic-liquid electrolyte; anode, Li metal.

(C) Cathode loading (mg cm−2) plotted versus discharge capacities (experimental and simulated values are plotted as squares and diamonds, respectively). A different loading changes the thickness of the cathode. The linear regression line corresponds to a carbon-specific capacity of 3,600 mAh gcarbon −1.
Simulated Performance Comparison of Composite Cathodes Based on CNPs and CNTs

(A–D) Simulation results for single-fold 1-cm-deep cell at various depths of discharge (DOD) in terms of (A and B) O2 concentration profiles and (C and D) Li2O2 thickness profiles in the cathode thickness direction. The two types of carbon considered for the composite cathodes are (A and C) CNTs and (B and D) CNPs.
Cycle-Life Tests and DEGC-MS Analysis
(A and B) Cycle number versus cell-scale specific energy (A), and discharge/charge cycle voltage profile (B) of the folded cell with a CNT composite cathode at 300 Wh kg−1 cell cutoff.
(C and D) Discharge/charge profile of the DEGC-MS cell (C) and O2 and CO2 gas production during charge (D).

head.ce:title: High-Energy-Density Li-O2 Battery at Cell Scale with Folded Cell Structure

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Energy Lab, Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology, Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd., 130 Samsung-ro, Suwon 16678, Republic of Korea
Analytical Engineering Group, Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology, Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd., 130 Samsung-ro, Suwon 16678, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology, Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As potential successors to intercalation-based battery systems, Li-air batteries have attracted enormous research attention because of their higher theoretical specific energy compared with conventional batteries, in terms of unit of energy per active material mass. However, this unit complicates the estimation of realistically attainable cell-scale energy densities because all other indispensable cell components are excluded. Here, we report Li-O2 batteries with an ultra-thin, customized gas-diffusion layer, highly conductive, stable polymeric ionic-liquid separator, and folded cell structure that reduce the volume and mass of multiple cell components. A complete cell-scale specific energy and energy density of 1,214 Wh kgcell −1 and 896 Wh Lcell −1, respectively, were experimentally achieved. Cell-scale electrochemical phenomena were simulated using a multiphysics model that included the oxygen flow caused by the cathode reaction, dynamic porosity changes, and resistance increase during Li2O2 formation. Both the cell geometry and cathode composition dramatically affected the cell's capacity.

Despite exponential advances in electronic devices, the energy density of their power sources has improved only 2- to 3-fold since the first appearance of Li-ion batteries, and their improvement has slowed as they reach their theoretical energy density. Recent expansion of electric vehicles with longer driving ranges demands a new battery chemistry beyond the current intercalation-based Li-ion battery. Recently, one non-intercalation battery system, the Li-O2 battery, has attracted intense research interest due to its ultra-high theoretical energy density of 3,458 Wh kg−1. There have been many conceptual studies on the Li-O2 batteries; however, the cell-scale energy densities have been rarely reported or, if reported, obtained with rough estimations not considering the actual weight of the cell components. Here, we demonstrate a full cell-scale Li-O2 battery with energy densities of 1,214 Wh kgcell −1 and 896 Wh Lcell −1 and discuss the factors that limit its energy densities.

A Li-O2 battery with folded cell structure achieved 1,214 Wh kgcell −1 and 896 Wh Lcell −1 with all the required battery cell components, from anode to GDL. Cell geometries and cathode components were controlled to investigate the impact of cell-scale and cathode-scale parameters on total cell performances. Multiphysics modeling and simulation were performed to study how the factors limiting energy density affect cell performances.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing demands for energy-storage systems capable of exceeding the energy densities of current Li-ion batteries have encouraged the investigation of new battery chemistries that can overcome the limitations of those based on the intercalation mechanism. 1–3 Among the many candidates for “beyond-intercalation” batteries, the Li-air battery, also known as the Li-O2 battery, has received widespread attention owing to its ultra-high theoretical specific energy of 3,458 Wh kg−1 (i.e., energy per the mass of Li2O2). Since the first introduction of the non-aqueous Li-O2 battery in 1996, 4 intense research has been conducted not only to understand the detailed chemistry of this system but also to achieve the theoretical energy density and longer cyclability. Although the details of many physicochemical phenomena have been elucidated, several critical issues such as the decomposition of cathode materials by corrosive superoxide radicals, singlet oxygen, and peroxide clusters remain to be resolved. 5–11

Various types of carbon and metal oxide catalysts, electrolytes, and additives have been reported as materials that can improve cathode performance demonstrating thousands of milliamp-hours of specific capacities with cycle-life stabilities exceeding approximately 1,000 cycles. 12–17 However, these improved numbers have mostly been described in terms of the energy per the mass of only the electron-conductive material, usually carbon, or Li2O2 in the cathode. This custom might have come from the reporting convention for intercalation-type batteries, which is based only on the mass of the active host material such as LiCoO2, and excludes those of other required components. Consequently, the actual cell-scale energy density and cyclability of the Li-O2 battery system have not been estimated with sufficient certainty. 1

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell Components, Structure, and Modeling Domains
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental cells were fabricated by first stacking lithium foil, a custom-made polymeric ionic liquid (PIL) separator, a porous carbon and ionic-liquid composite cathode, and the GDL, followed by folding, as shown in Figures 1A, 1C, and 1D.

For the cathode, the masses of the electrolyte and binder were minimized without compromising cell performance. The properties of the ionic liquid used for the cathode electrolyte satisfied the requirements of high ionic conductivity, O2 solubility, O2 diffusivity, chemical and electrochemical stability, and low volatility at the cell operating temperature, 80°C. Nanosized carbon was ideal for use as a cathode electron-conductive material owing to its high electrical conductivity, large surface area, and low density. In contrast, most metal oxide catalysts are typically too heavy to realize high cell-scale specific energy. Furthermore, the cathode must be sufficiently thin to ensure its maximum usage over the entire cathode volume. The requirements for the separator—high Li-ion conductivity, low volatility, and toughness to prevent lithium dendrite penetration while maintaining the minimum mass and volume—were partly met by using a PIL-impregnated polypropylene separator. The custom-modified GDL exhibited high electrical conductivity and high oxygen flux, while possessing a minimized volume and mass. All cells were fabricated and tested in a dry room (dew point −60°C) to eliminate undesired toroidal-type Li2O2 products and corrosive side reactions. 22–24 Scanning electron microscopy images before and after discharge confirmed that no toroidal reaction products were formed, although electrolyte was visibly extruded from the cathode (Figure S1).

The Li-O2 cell-scale modeling domain is shown in Figure 1B, with the governing physical principles in each domain. The GDL, cathode, separator, and anode were placed in order from top to bottom as they were stacked in the experimental cell. Electrolyte extrusion from the cathode due to the formation of reaction products inside the cathode was not considered in this model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon Nanoparticle Composite Cathode: GDL Thickness and Cell Depth Effects
ce:section.ce:para: Figures 2A and 2B show the discharge curves of folded cells with a length (L) of 5 cm and depths (D) of 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 cm obtained from the experiments and simulations, respectively. The GDL used for this experiment was SGL 25BA with a thickness of 180 μm and areal density of 2 mg cm−2. The cathodes were composite electrodes comprising carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) (diameter 20 nm, Figure S2A) and an ionic liquid, 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide (EMITFSI), having a thickness of 30 μm and, thus, a loading of 5 mg cm−2. The initial electrolyte porosity was 0.86 with no measurable void volume, assuming a CNP density of 2.25 g mL−1. 25 The areal density and thickness of the separator were 3 mg cm−2 and 20 μm, respectively. The thickness of the lithium anode was 30 μm. The total height (H) of the folded cell was 340 μm.

Figure 2C shows plots of the experimental (from Figure 2A, red squares) and simulated (from Figure 2B, blue diamonds) discharge capacities obtained at 2.3 V. The precision, expressed as the root-mean-square deviation (RMSD) between the experimental and simulated capacities, is 4.1. The straight line in Figure 2C represents the carbon-specific discharge capacity, i.e., 3,200 mAh gcarbon −1. The cell with a depth of 3 cm achieves a carbon-specific capacity of 3,148 mAh gcarbon −1, cell-specific energy of 338 Wh kgcell −1, and energy density of 376 Wh Lcell −1. For the cell-scale specific energy and energy density calculations, all masses and volumes from the GDL to the lithium anode were included. The proportional increase in the discharge capacity with the increasing depth of the cell can be interpreted as due to the high oxygen flux and evenly distributed current within the cell up to a depth of 3 cm.

The specific energy and energy density were enhanced to attain their theoretical maxima by systematically engineering the GDL thickness as well as the cell geometry, with iterative assistance from the simulation results.

Because the GDL simultaneously functions as both the GDL and the current collector, its geometry (e.g., thickness) and material properties (e.g., porosity and conductivity) can directly affect electron and O2 flux and, consequently, cell electrochemical performance. Figures 3A and 3B show the experimental and simulated discharge curves of the folded cells with depths of 0.5, 1, 2, 3, and 8 cm, respectively. A custom-modified carbon-felt GDL with a thickness of 50 μm was used to increase the energy density of the cells. Other than the GDL, all other components (cathode, separator, and anode) were the same as described for the experimental and simulation setups in Figure 2.

Cells with depths of 0.5–3 cm exhibit linearly increasing capacities with increasing depths. The same trend can be seen with the 180-μm-thick GDL in Figure 2. The discharge curve of the cell with a depth of 8 cm shows a high overpotential of 80 mV at the beginning of discharge, which continues until the discharge reaches 70 mAh. Subsequently, the overpotential gradually increases the dropping potential from 2.6 to 2.3 V until the capacity reaches 180 mAh, followed by an exponential potential drop to eventually reach the discharge stop condition at 2.2 V. The simulation results in Figure 3B reproduce the overall experimental behavior of the cells with the modified 50-μm GDL with depths from 0.5 to 8 cm.

Figure 3C shows the discharge capacities obtained at 2.3 V from experiments (Figure 3A, red squares) and simulations (Figure 3B, blue diamonds). The RMSD between the experimental and simulated capacities is 0.82. The straight line plotted in Figure 3C represents the carbon-specific discharge capacity of 3,200 mAh gcarbon −1 and is the same as that in Figure 2C. The results indicate that the thinner custom-modified GDL, which might be expected to hinder oxygen flow and increase the electrical overpotential, does not affect the carbon-specific energy density up to a cell depth of 3 cm. With a cell depth of 3 cm, a cell-specific energy of 700 Wh kgcell −1 and volume-specific energy density of 610 Wh Lcell −1 are achieved, whereas for a cell depth of 8 cm, the specific energy and energy densities decrease to 580 Wh kgcell −1 and 520 Wh Lcell −1, respectively.

Figure 4A shows the simulated oxygen mass fractions (
ω
O 2
) in the GDL and oxygen concentrations (
C
O 2
) in the electrolyte at 0%, 20%, 50%, 85%, and 100% depths of discharge (DOD, from top to bottom) for cells with depths of 2 cm (left) and 8 cm (right). This figure resembles Figure 1B, although only the GDL and cathode are shown. The left side wall of the GDL domain (upper layer) is the oxygen inlet and the bottom wall is in contact with the separator. The thickness of the GDL here is 25 μm, which is one symmetrical half of the total GDL thickness of 50 μm. With a cathode thickness of 30 μm, the total thickness of the GDL and cathode is 55 μm. Oxygen consumption due to the discharge reaction results in decreased oxygen concentration at the cathode. For a cell depth of 8 cm, at the beginning of discharge, most oxygen depletion occurs on the left side (near the oxygen inlet) of the cathode at 0%–20% DOD. Thereafter, the depletion zone moves to deeper cell regions to consume the fresh carbon surfaces with less Li2O2 film while gradually increasing the total resistance of the cell (Figures 3A and 3B). At a DOD of 100%, approximately 6 cm of the cathode is passivated by Li2O2 formation. This increases the oxygen transport distance through the GDL and causes oxygen depletion deep inside the cell, thus rapidly increasing the concentration overpotential at the end of discharge. Oxygen depletion is not observed in the 2-cm-deep cell.

Figure 4B shows the magnitude of the electric current density, which is directed along the arrow shown in the GDL, cathode, and anode; it also shows the magnitude of the electrolyte current density in the separator at 100% DOD. The directions of the electrolyte current density vectors are not shown as they are all upward and carry no information. The current is not evenly distributed and flows via a pathway along which the electric conductivity is maximized, i.e., over fresh carbon surfaces. The difference in resistance between the GDL and lithium anode leads to this uneven current distribution during the discharge process. The current tends to flow across the shortest possible distance through the more resistive GDL, thereby strengthening the current flow near the oxygen inlet side (on the left) and producing Li2O2 at the initiation of discharge. This uneven current distribution and oxygen depletion eventually prevent the carbon from utilizing its full capacity and results in less carbon-specific capacity in the cell than 3,200 mAh gcarbon −1 with a depth of 8 cm (Figure 3C).

In sum, when the GDL thickness and discharge current density are fixed, there is a certain cell depth where a uniform electric current and sufficient oxygen flux are maintained such that the whole volume of the cathode is utilized. For example, when a 50-μm-thick GDL and 0.24 mA cm−2 discharge current are used for cell operation, the depth of the cell should be less than 6 cm to ensure full utilization of its cathode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Maximum Cell-Scale Capacity Prediction for CNP Cathode
ce:section.ce:para: Figures 5A and 5B show the energy density and specific energy with varying cell depths and GDL thicknesses. For this calculation, the 2-mm space shown in Figure 1B is included in the overall cell volume. This space is assumed to be the minimum required width of the gas channel between cells when they are closely stacked in a battery module. To maximize the energy density of the cell this space needs to be optimized in the module and packing design, which is beyond the scope of this research. The inclusion of this space in the cell design results in cells with depths of less than 2 cm having gradually reduced energy densities.

A smaller cell depth produces larger errors between the experimental and simulated values because the small values of the energy densities are divided by the small values of the volume and mass; nevertheless, the degree of the experimental errors remains unchanged. Thus, in complete cells, volumetric and gravimetric energy densities of 600 Wh L−1 and over 700 Wh kg−1, respectively, are experimentally achieved by using a cathode consisting of a CNP/ionic-liquid composite, PIL separator, custom-modified GDL, and folded cell structure. The simulated results support the experimental data and also provide a guideline for the maximum theoretical values that could possibly be achieved by a Li2O2 battery cell.

Thus, when the GDL becomes thinner, the achievable energy densities increase because of the reduced total cell volume and mass, as can be seen in Figure 5. However, due to hindered oxygen flux and reduced conductivity, the optimized cell depth, in which all the cathode volume can be utilized homogenously, decreases as the GDL thickness decreases. For example, for a cell with a 50-μm-thick GDL, 600 Wh L−1 can be achieved up to 6 cm depth, whereas the cell with a 20-μm-thick GDL can achieve 750 Wh L−1 only up to 4 cm depth, and the energy density decreases rapidly as the depth of the cell exceeds 4 cm.

Gray solid lines and dotted lines in Figure 5 are the calculated theoretical limits when Li2O2 formation is limited by cathode pore volume or by surface passivation, respectively, for a cell with a 50-μm-thick GDL.

Assuming that at least two layers of 10-μm carbon fibers would be required to fabricate a mechanically stable GDL construction, i.e., a GDL thickness of 20 μm, the highest volumetric and gravimetric energy densities of a Li2O2 battery cell with CNPs should be 750 Wh L−1 and 700 Wh kg−1, respectively, with a cell depth of less than 4 cm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CNT Composite Cathode: Effect of Cathode Composition
ce:section.ce:para: We increased the energy density even further by introducing carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and further reducing the amount of electrolyte in the composite cathode. The composite cathode consists of CNTs and 1 M LiTFSI in EMITFSI with a weight ratio of 1:2, with 2% polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) as the binder. The initial electrolyte and void porosities of the cathodes were 0.43 and 0.14, assuming a CNT (diameter 10 nm, 3 walls, Figure S2B) density of 0.8 g mL−1. 26 Our previous work indicated that a carbon composite with an aspect ratio this high can produce a larger number of voids and a surface area capable of embedding additional Li2O2 and enhancing the O2 flux in the cathode. 27

Figure 6A shows the discharge curves of the cells with the CNT composite cathodes. Cells (D = 1 cm, L = 3 cm) with different cathode loadings were investigated to study the effect of the cathode thickness on cell performance. The cathode loadings were 1.5, 2.0, and 3.0 mg cm−2, and increase as the areal capacity (mA cm−2) increases. The simulated discharge curves also show the same capacity values for the 1.5–3 mg cm−2 loadings (Figure 6B).

Figure 6C plots the discharge capacities obtained at 2.2 V from experiments (Figure 6A) and simulations (Figure 6B); the straight line represents a carbon-specific discharge capacity of 3,600 mAh gcarbon −1. For the cell with a 3 mg cm−2 cathode loading (L = 3 cm, D = 1 cm, and H = 210 μm), a carbon-specific capacity of 3,550 mAh gcarbon −1, cell-specific energy of 1,214 Wh kgcell −1, and energy density of 896 Wh Lcell −1 were achieved experimentally. Considering the 30 μm of Li thickness, 57% of the lithium anode was consumed in the 3 mg cm−2-loaded cell.

Proportional increases in the discharge capacities with the increasing thickness of the cathodes are attributed to a high oxygen flux and evenly distributed current within the cathode up to a 3 mg cm−2 loading. The simulation results predict that a CNT loading of 6 mg cm−2 on the cathode would not utilize the entire volume as efficiently as the cells with lower cathode loadings (Figure 6C).

Microscopic capacity limitation factors were studied by comparing two different composite cathodes (CNP- and CNT-based) in cells with D = 1 cm to eliminate macroscopic capacity limitation effects. In general, a lower electrolyte/cathode mass ratio with a higher surface-area carbon gives a higher cathode specific capacity owing to the reduced total mass per reaction surface area. However, different limiting factors could arise for different cathode compositions.

Figures 7A and 7B show the simulated oxygen concentration profiles and Figures 7C and 7D the simulated Li2O2 thicknesses along with the cathode thickness at various DODs. Plots in Figures 7A and 7C present the results for cells with a 15-μm-thick CNT composite cathode (2 mg cm−2), whereas Figures 7B and 7D are those for the 30-μm-thick CNP composite cathode (5 mg cm−2). In Figures 7A and 7C, the oxygen concentration decreases to zero at 100% DOD, even though the cathode is only 15 μm thick, because of the high geometric surface area of the CNTs (500 m2 g−1) and a low porosity (0.44). The pores in the cathode are fully occupied and clogged by the produced Li2O2, which, although reaching a thickness of 2.8 nm, is still much less than the known thickness limit of ∼10 nm. 28–30 However, in the CNP composite cathode (Figures 7B and 7D), which offers ample space for Li2O2 formation with its high porosity of 0.88, the smaller geometric surface area of the CNPs (133 m2 g−1) shows no oxygen depletion within the cell even at 100% DOD, indicating that the main capacity limitation originates from Li2O2 surface coating of 9 nm and reaction surface passivation.

The results imply that the cell capacity could be limited by both clogging and Li2O2 surface passivation, which exert variable influence according to the cathode composition and geometric parameters such as porosity and surface area.

Thus, for the cathode with the higher surface-area carbon and lower porosity, the maximum capacity is determined by the porosity volume and limited by the clogging of the pores. Considering the low porosity (0.44) and high specific surface area (500 m2 gcarbon −1) of the CNT cathode, the calculated maximum carbon-specific capacity for the pore volume is 3,552 mAh gcarbon −1, which closely approximates the straight line shown in Figure 6C. The slightly higher experimental value of about 1.5% could be due to slight expansion of the cathode during discharge process. With the lower surface-area carbon and higher porosity, the limiting thickness of the Li2O2 formed on the reaction surface area determines the maximum capacity and the limitation comes from surface passivation. Considering the high porosity (0.88) and low specific surface area (133 m2 gcarbon −1) of the CNP cathode, the calculated maximum carbon-specific capacity for 9-nm grown Li2O2 is 3,238 mAh gcarbon −1, which is also very close to the straight lines in Figures 2C and 3C. All the calculated volume ratios of the components in CNT and CNP cathodes as well as theoretical capacity limits by pore volume clogging and Li2O2 surface passivation are tabulated in Table S3.
Based on the foregoing experiments and simulations, a complete optimized cell comprising a CNT cathode, custom-modified GDL, and PIL separator was constructed and subjected to cycle-life testing.

Figures 8A and 8B show the results of the cycle-life tests in which the single-fold cell was cycled eight times at a 300 Wh kgcell −1 cutoff. Although this is the best cycle number with this high cell-scale specific energy as far as we know, more research on cycle stability is needed for this system.

Figures 8C and 8D show differential electrochemical gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy (DEGC-MS) results, whereby the pressure drop during discharge and gas production during charge were measured. The linearly increasing O2 consumption during discharge corresponding to two-electrons-transfer per O2 (Figure S3) and experimental discharge voltage of 2.65 V without additional discharge plateau due to Li2CO3 confirm Li2O2 as the discharge product. Ex situ X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis result also supports formation of Li2O2 (Figure S4). However, the increasing CO2 and decreasing O2 gas production at the end of the charging stage (over 3.8 V) explain the low cycle stability. Because Li2CO3 is not a direct discharge product, detected CO2 is attributed to Li2CO3 formed by carbonaceous cathode material decomposition. This also suggests that the decomposition mechanism of this system might not be significantly different from a system with an organic electrolyte, which would usually show CO2 gas production at high potentials. 31,32

Recently, the degradation mechanisms operative in this battery system with an organic electrolyte were extensively studied. 24,33,34 We believe that there is immense potential with respect to stabilizing the corrosive reaction intermediates and improving cycle life without compromising the total cell energy density. Furthermore, recent research on inorganic electrolytes is opening new research domains that can fundamentally circumvent the corrosive reactions observed with organic electrolytes. 35,36

The use of an innovative folded cell design integrating high-surface-area CNTs, an optimized electrolyte/carbon ratio in the cathode, an electrochemically stable high Li-ion conductive PIL separator, and an ultra-thin custom-made GDL enabled us to achieve complete cell-scale specific energy and energy densities as high as 1,214 Wh kgcell −1 and 896 Wh Lcell −1, respectively. The performance of the cell was proven experimentally and by simulation that included the oxygen gas flow in the GDL by considering the pressure reduction caused by the cathode reaction with dynamic porosity and reaction surface resistance changes during the discharge reaction. The simulations enabled the cell dimensions and conditions to be varied, thereby facilitating optimization of the cell depth and improving our understanding of the causes of capacity limitations.

When the depth of the folded cell with a 50-μm GDL exceeded 6 cm, the uneven usage of the cathode in the depth direction and oxygen depletion deep inside the cell were the main cell-scale capacity-limiting factors; these result from the mismatch between the electric conductivities of the GDL and the anode. When the depth was less than 6 cm, oxygen depletion and surface passivation inside the cathode were the main cathode-scale capacity-limiting factors, which were caused by pore clogging and active surface coating, respectively, by the produced Li2O2. The reaction surface area and porosity of the cathode controlled which factor dominated cell death.

In sum, on the cell scale, a thinner GDL will reduce the weight and volume of the cell and consequently improve the overall cell energy densities. However, the reduction of the conductivity and O2 flux due to thinning of the GDL will limit the optimized cell depth to a certain length. On the cathode scale, a high surface reaction area and optimized porosity, which enable increased surface area per unit mass with enough space for Li2O2 formation, will lead to maximum cell energy densities. These results will be used in future as fundamental guidelines for the module and pack designs of Li-air battery systems.

The anode comprised a 30-μm-thick Li metal foil on a 10-μm-thick Cu substrate (Honjo Metal, Japan), used without further treatment. This readily removable Cu substrate in the multi-folded cell was not factored into the energy density calculations.

The separator consisted of an ionic-liquid gel polymer electrolyte impregnated in a porous polypropylene separator film. First, the PIL, poly(diallyldimethylammonium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide), was synthesized by an anion exchange reaction between a solution of poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDADMAC; Sigma-Aldrich, MO) with lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI, 200% by weight versus PDADMAC; Sigma-Aldrich). The detailed synthetic procedure can be found elsewhere. 37 Next, the PIL (3.9 g) was mixed with N-methyl-N-butylpyrrolidinium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (16.6 g, PYR14TFSI; Sigma-Aldrich) and LiTFSI (1.7 g) in acetonitrile (8.4 g, Sigma-Aldrich). The prepared solution was then applied to one side of the porous polypropylene separator (G0910A, Asahi) using a doctor blade to control the thickness of the film followed by drying at 80°C under vacuum for 4 hr. The same procedure was conducted on the other side to obtain the final film. The thickness of the resulting electrolyte layer was 20 μm and the areal weight was 2.8 mg cm−2.

The cathode was prepared by combining composites of carbon, an ionic-liquid electrolyte, and binder. Two types of carbon, carbon nanoparticles (CNPs, Printex XE2-B; Degussa, Germany) or single-walled CNTs (Elicarb; Thomas Swan, UK), respectively, were mixed with the ionic-liquid electrolyte, 0.5 M LiTFSI in EMITFSI (Sigma-Aldrich), and PTFE beads as the binder. The mixing ratios by mass of the carbon, ionic liquid, and binder were 1:5:0.2 and 1:2:0.2 for the composites prepared using CNPs and CNTs, respectively. The composite cathodes were roll-pressed to a thickness of 30 μm. The areal density of the cathode containing the CNPs was 5 mg cm−2, and those based on the CNTs were controlled to 1.5, 2, and 3 mg cm−2, to investigate the effect of the cathode thickness on cell performance.

Two types of GDL were used in this study. First, a carbon paper GDL (GDL 25 BA; SGL Group, Germany) with a thickness of 180 μm was used as purchased to verify the simulation by changing the depths (D) of the folded cells. Second, to achieve high volumetric and gravimetric specific energies, a carbon-felt GDL (GDL 29 BA; SGL Group) was modified by scraping the surfaces and hot-pressing at 80°C for 4 hr.

An area of 2.5 × 3.5 cm2 of the prepared separator was attached to an area of 2 × 3 cm2 of the Li anodes. Two 1 × 3 cm2 sheets of the prepared cathodes were placed in parallel on top of the separator. The prepared 1.5 × 3 cm2 GDL was positioned on one of the cathode sheets and the cell was folded as shown in Figures 1A, 1C, and 1D. Both sides of the cell were sealed by polyimide films (3M, MN, USA). For the depth-dependence experiments, cells with a length of 5 cm (Figure 1A, L) were fabricated.

The cell was connected to a custom-built jig and placed in a 20-L reaction chamber in which the temperature and atmosphere were controlled at 80°C and 100% O2 (99.999%, Praxair, CT, USA), respectively, at 1 atm. The battery performance was tested using a charge/discharge station (Toyo, Japan). Discharge was applied in the constant current mode with a specific current density of 0.24 mA cm−2 in the cathode area. All cell fabrications and tests were conducted in a dry room (dew point −60°C) to eliminate undesired toroidal-type Li2O2 products and corrosive side reactions. 22–24 The water contents of all cell components were checked by Karl-Fisher Coulombic titration (851 Titrando; Metrohm, Switzerland), and found to be less than 20 ppm. The crystalline phases of the cells were characterized by XRD (Empyrean; Malvern Panalytical, UK) operated at 40 kV and 40 mA with Cu Kα radiation (= 1.54 Å). The scan rate was 6° per minute in 0.02° increments of 2θ from 10° to 90° (Figure S4). DEGC-MS measurements were conducted with a gas chromatograph/mass spectrometer (HPR-20; Hiden Analytical, UK) and potentio-galvanostat (1287A; Solartron) using a custom-made cell setup with a punched unfolded CNT cell. A more detailed DEGC-MS setup can be found elsewhere. 38

The thicknesses of the components were measured with an optical microscope (DSX510; Olympus, Japan) and a precision thickness gauge (FT3-V; Hanatek, Korea). The areal densities of the GDL, cathode, and separator sheets were measured, and then the masses and volumes were calculated using the measured thicknesses and pre-determined dimensions (L and D). The anode volumes and masses were also calculated with the known density of lithium (1.42 g mL−1), measured thickness, and pre-determined dimensions (L and D).

A two-dimensional simulation using the Comsol Multiphysics package was employed to investigate the symmetric nature of cell operation. The model incorporates the oxygen gas flow in the GDL, dissolution of O2 into the electrolyte, electrochemistry across all cell components, dynamically changing carbon surfaces, and porosity evolution due to Li2O2 formation. Details are described in Supplemental Information as “Supplemental Experimental Information: Modeling and Simulations.”

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Dr. Min Sik Park for his contribution to this project. This work was supported by funds from Samsung Electronics.

ce:section.ce:para: H.C.L. and D.I. conceived the idea. J.O.P. fabricated the cathode. M.K. synthesized the PIL. K.H.C. and M.K. fabricated the separator. H.C.L. performed the modeling and simulations. J.-H.K., M.K., and J.O.P. assembled the cells and performed electrochemical characterizations. H.J.K. performed the DEGC-MS analysis. K.K. performed XRD analysis. H.C.L. wrote the manuscript. D.I. supervised the project. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.
A relevant patent application is in progress by Samsung Electronics.
Supplemental Information includes Supplemental Experimental Procedures, four figures, and three tables and can be found with this article online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joule.2018.11.016.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Document S1. Supplemental Experimental Procedures, Figures S1–S4, and Tables S1–S3
Document S2. Article plus Supplemental Information
